title: Chapter 9: The Interrogation
content: Darius found the traitor before noon.

It was one of the gate guards — a young wolf named Corvin who had been stationed at the eastern post the night before the rogue attack. Corvin's body was discovered in the barracks, still warm, his throat cut with a blade that had been placed in his own hand to make it look like suicide.
"It wasn't suicide," Darius reported, standing in Kael's study with his arms crossed and his grey eyes blazing. "The angle of the wound is wrong. Someone killed him and staged the scene."
"Someone who wanted to cover their tracks." Kael's voice was ice. "Convenient."

*"Convenient and OBVIOUS. Isolde's fingerprints are all over this — metaphorically speaking. She's too smart to leave actual fingerprints. But this has her STYLE written all over it. Frame the pawn, kill the pawn, leave nothing connecting back to the queen."*
Sera stood by the window, Kael's coat still wrapped around her shoulders. She hadn't changed out of it yet. She wasn't sure she wanted to.
"Corvin was young," she said quietly. "Barely twenty. He wouldn't have done this on his own."
"Someone paid him. Or threatened him." Kael's jaw tightened. "Either way, he was a tool. And tools get discarded when they're no longer useful."
"Lady Vane was in the noble wing all night," Darius said. "A dozen witnesses can confirm it."

"Of course they can. She's not stupid enough to get her own hands dirty." Kael rose from his desk. "But someone in her household knows something. Someone ran messages. Someone paid the guard. Someone — "
"I'll find them." Sera's voice was steady. "Let me help with the interrogations."
Both men turned to look at her.
"You're not a warrior," Darius said, not unkindly. "Interrogations can be... unpleasant."
"I'm not going to threaten anyone." Sera met Kael's eyes. "But I can hear things. Things people don't say out loud."

Kael's expression flickered. He knew what she meant. The mind-link worked on him, but they hadn't tested whether it worked on other wolves. Whether the Moon-Marked gift was specific to her connection with Kael, or whether it was broader than that.
"You want to try reading other wolves," he said.

"I want to try *listening*. If it works — if I can hear even fragments — then I can tell which servants are lying and which ones are just scared." Sera took a breath. "And if it doesn't work, I'm still the woman who transformed into a silver wolf and killed two rogues this morning. They'll be too busy being afraid of me to lie effectively."
Darius made a sound that might have been approval. "She's not wrong."
Kael studied her for a long moment. "Fine. But Darius goes with you. And if any of them so much as twitch in your direction — "
"I know. You'll kill them horribly." Sera's smile was small but real. "Your inner wolf already has a detailed plan involving their bones and a chew toy."

*"SHE KNOWS ABOUT THE CHEW TOY LINE. I made that line WEEKS ago. She's been listening THAT LONG. To EVERYTHING. And she STILL kissed us. She still — okay. Okay, I'm calm. I'm the calmest inner wolf. I'm going to help with interrogations by providing MORAL SUPPORT from inside Kael's head."*
The servants' hall was packed.

Twenty-three members of the castle staff — maids, cooks, stable hands, the wolves who kept the fortress running — stood in uneasy clusters as Sera entered with Darius at her back. The Beta's presence was a statement: *the Alpha's authority is behind this wolf, and questioning her is questioning him.*
"Thank you for coming," Sera said, her voice carrying through the hall with a confidence she didn't entirely feel. "I know you're all afraid. The rogue attack this morning has everyone on edge."
A few nods. More silence.

"I'm not here to accuse anyone. I'm here to listen." She walked slowly through the crowd, making eye contact with each wolf in turn. "One of your coworkers — Corvin — is dead. Someone used him to let the rogues into the castle, and then someone killed him to cover their tracks. That someone is probably in this room."
The tension ratcheted up several notches.

"So I'm going to ask each of you a simple question: did you see or hear anything unusual last night? Anything at all. Even if it seemed insignificant." She stopped in front of a young kitchen maid — the same one who had flinched away from her three days ago. "Let's start with you."
The maid's eyes were wide with terror. "I — I didn't see anything. I swear. I was in the kitchens all night, preparing the morning bread — "

She was telling the truth. Sera couldn't *hear* her thoughts the way she could hear Kael's — the link was specific to him — but she could feel the texture of the girl's emotions. Fear. Honesty. Relief that she actually had an alibi.
"Good. Thank you."
She moved on. A stable hand. A laundress. Two more kitchen workers. Each one radiated fear but nothing else. No guilt. No deception.
Then she stopped in front of a footman — middle-aged, nervous, sweating more than the cool morning air justified.
"And you?"
"I was in the servants' quarters all night, miss. Same as always."
The lie hit Sera like a physical blow.

She couldn't hear his thoughts, but she could *feel* the wrongness — a sour, curdled quality to his emotions that was completely different from the clean fear of the others. He was terrified, yes, but it was the terror of someone who had something to hide.
"Is that so?" Sera kept her voice pleasant. "Because the night guard saw someone matching your description near the eastern gate around midnight. Are you sure you weren't taking a walk?"
The footman's face went white. "I — I might have stepped out for some air — "
"Darius." Sera didn't raise her voice. "This one."
Darius's hand closed on the footman's shoulder like a steel trap. "Let's take a walk, shall we? Somewhere more... private."
The footman's nerve broke. "It wasn't me! I didn't let them in! I just — Lady Vane's steward paid me to deliver a message to Corvin! I didn't know what it said! I swear on the Moon, I didn't know!"
The hall went absolutely silent.
"Lady Vane's steward," Sera repeated. "The message. What did it look like?"

"A sealed letter. With her crest. I didn't open it — I'm not stupid enough to open a noble's letter — but Corvin read it and went pale." The footman was babbling now, words tumbling over each other. "Then he told me to forget I'd ever delivered anything. And then this morning he was dead and I — I was too scared to say anything — "
Sera turned to face the hall. "Does anyone else have information about last night? Anyone who received instructions, messages, or payments from Lady Vane's household?"
A long pause.

Then, slowly, a young laundress raised her hand. "I... I was told to leave the eastern servant's door unlocked. Lady Vane's steward said it was for a 'surprise inspection.' I didn't question it — he's a noble's steward, you don't question noble stewards — "
Another hand went up. A stable boy. "I was told to saddle a horse for a messenger at midnight. The steward said it was urgent pack business."

The floodgates opened. Four more servants came forward with similar stories — minor tasks, seemingly innocuous, all ordered by Isolde's steward, all contributing to the security breach that had nearly killed the Alpha.

Isolde had built her conspiracy out of small, deniable pieces. No single servant had done anything overtly treasonous. No single order had been obviously criminal. It was a masterwork of plausible deniability — and it had almost worked.
"Darius," Sera said quietly, "arrest the steward. And put Lady Vane under house arrest pending a full investigation."
Darius's eyebrows rose. "That's the Alpha's authority, not mine."
"Then I'll get his authority." Sera turned to go, then paused. "But I'm fairly certain I know what he's going to say."
She was right.
"Arrest them both," Kael said, his voice flat with barely controlled fury. "The steward for conspiracy. Isolde for — "

"We don't have enough for Isolde," Sera said. "Not yet. She's too careful. The steward will take the fall — he's paid to take the fall — and she'll claim ignorance. 'A rogue steward acting without my knowledge. I'm horrified. I had no idea.'"
Kael's fist slammed against his desk. "I should have exiled her years ago."

"You couldn't. Her family is too powerful. Half the noble houses would have revolted." Sera moved to stand beside him. "But now she's made a mistake. She's attacked the Alpha openly — or at least, her people have. The nobles who supported her out of political convenience are going to rethink that support when they realize she's willing to risk the entire pack's safety for a personal vendetta."

*"She's right. She's ALWAYS right. How is she always right? Is this a Moon-Marked thing or is it just a Sera thing? I think it's a Sera thing. She's strategic and brilliant and she just interrogated twenty-three servants and found the traitor in under an hour and I — I need to tell her. I need to say it. The word. The one I couldn't say this morning."*
Sera's heart caught.
"What word?" she asked softly.
Kael went still. "I didn't say anything."
"You were thinking it."
The silence between them was thick with unspoken things. Outside the window, the morning sun had given way to grey clouds. A storm was coming.

"The word," Kael said, his voice rough, "is one I've never said to anyone. Not to my family. Not to my pack. Not even to myself." He turned to face her, golden eyes burning. "I was raised to believe that Alphas don't need anyone. That the Wolf King stands alone. That love — " the word cracked in his throat — "that love is a weakness. A vulnerability. Something that can be exploited."
"It's not a weakness." Sera's voice was barely a whisper.

"I know." Kael's hand rose to cup her face. "I know that now. Because you've been in my head for weeks, hearing every ridiculous thought, every embarrassing confession, every moment of weakness — and instead of using it against me, you *stayed*. You protected me. You kissed me. You sat in my lap while I was paralyzed and stared down three traitors without flinching."
"Technically I was also paralyzed at the time," Sera said. "Just in a different way."
Kael's laugh was ragged. "You're impossible."
"I've been told."
He pulled her closer. "I don't know how to say it. The word. I don't know how to be... *that*. But I want to learn. With you. If you'll have me."

*"KAEL. KAEL, YOU'RE DOING IT. YOU'RE ACTUALLY DOING IT. I'M SO PROUD OF YOU. I'M SO — okay, I'm crying again. Happy tears. All the happy tears. Someone get me a tissue. Inner wolves can't use tissues. I'll just cry internally. CRYING INTERNALLY WITH JOY."*
Sera rose on her toes and pressed her lips to his. Gentle this time. Reassuring.
"You don't have to say it yet," she murmured against his mouth. "I can hear it anyway."
She tapped her temple.
"Every ridiculous word."
